Further calls upon the Myanmar army to fully respect and protect the human rights of all persons in Myanmar, including the Rohingya and other persons belonging to ethnic and religious minorities, to refrain from excessive use of force, to exercise the utmost restraint and to seek a peaceful solution to the crisis, and recalls its responsibility to respect democratic principles and its obligation under international human rights law to respect the rule of law and human rights, including the right to life, the right to freedom of peaceful assembly and association and the right to freedom of opinion and expression, including the freedom to seek, receive and impart information, and that the use of torture and other cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment is prohibited;

11. Expresses deep concern at the continuing reports of serious human rights violations and abuses committed in Myanmar by the military and security forces, as well as violations of international humanitarian law, in particular against Rohingya and other persons belonging to ethnic and religious minorities, including arbitrary arrest, death in detention, torture and other cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment, mutilation and intentional killing of children, the recruitment and use of children in armed conflict and for forced labour or other forms of work, the use of schools and universities for military purposes in violation of international law, attacks on schools and universities, hospitals and places of worship, as well as persons who are protected because they are in such institutions, indiscriminate shelling of civilian areas, destruction, including by fire, housing, deprivation of economic, social and cultural rights, forced displacement in Bangladesh of more than 923,000 persons, forced rape of and other forms of violence, and exploitation of sexual minorities, and other forms of violence;

12. Also expresses its deep concern at reports of human rights violations and violations, including violations and violations related to the conflict, committed against the Rohingya in the Rakhine State and of reports that Rohingya and other persons belonging to ethnic and religious minorities are forcibly recruited by the Myanmar army;

13. Urges the Myanmar army to cease immediately all airstrikes, the use of anti-personnel landmines and the use of arson, and condemns in the strongest terms the attacks and killings that have claimed the lives of civilians throughout Myanmar, as well as all attacks against civilians and civilian infrastructure and indiscriminate attacks;

14. Condemns in the strongest terms the indiscriminate attacks by the Myanmar army on children, and calls upon all parties, in particular the Myanmar army, to put an end to violations and abuses committed against children, including the six grave violations of the rights of the child in armed conflict, to ensure that perpetrators of such violations and abuses are held accountable, to ensure the protection of all children in armed conflict, including by arresting and preventing the recruitment of children into the armed forces and by ensuring the immediate and safe release of those recruited, and to give survivors access to adequate support, including education, psychosocial and psychological support, and to justice and redress;

15. Calls upon all parties to the conflict in Myanmar, in particular the military, to cease all attacks against schools, universities and other educational institutions, as well as attacks against students, teachers and other educational personnel, in violation of international humanitarian law, to investigate and prosecute such attacks, as appropriate, and to mitigate and avoid, as appropriate, the use of schools by the armed forces, including by implementing Security Council resolution 2601 (2021) of 29 October 2021, noting in this regard the relevance of the Declaration on Security in Schools, and adopting further measures to that end, including considering the implementation of the Guidelines for the Protection of Schools and Universities from Military Use in Armed Conflict, creating safe, non-violent, inclusive and supportive learning environments and ensuring quality education for all;

16. Calls upon the Myanmar army to end arbitrary and unlawful arrests and detentions of children, to release immediately and unconditionally all children detained in interrogation centres and prisons and to ensure their rehabilitation and reintegration into their families and communities;

17. Calls upon all parties to the conflict in Myanmar, in particular the military, to put an immediate end to violence, including sexual and gender-based violence, and all violations of international law, including international human rights law and international humanitarian law, as well as human rights violations committed in Myanmar, to hold perpetrators of violations and abuses accountable, including through independent and impartial national procedures for victims and survivors to obtain justice and redress, respect and protect civilians, to enable humanitarian relief to access affected populations, to exercise restraint, to end the conflict and to demonstrate their readiness to resume dialogue and constitutional reform;

18. Calls upon the Myanmar army to cease attacking citizens of Myanmar who are outside the country on the grounds that they have exercised their right to freedom of expression, including the waiver of passport cancellation, surveillance and harassment of the persons concerned and the use of the threat against their family members who remain in Myanmar;

19. Also calls upon the Myanmar army to cease attacks on leaders and trade unions on the grounds that they have exercised their freedom of association, including the cessation of arbitrary arrests, detention, torture, intimidation, supervision of workers who attempt to negotiate wage increases and deprivation of fundamental civil liberties, procedural guarantees and access to remedies;

20. Expresses deep concern at the announcement by the Myanmar Army that all men between the ages of 18 and 35 and all women between the ages of 18 and 27 could be subject to compulsory conscription and at reports that, in order to force recruitment, it has committed abductions, threatened communities with burning their villages, offered money, food and access to citizenship to young Rohingya and threatened with repression;

21. Calls for the early commencement of a constructive, inclusive and peaceful dialogue among all parties, in accordance with the will and interests of the people of Myanmar, with a view to restoring democratic governance;

22. Urges Myanmar to take all measures within its power, in accordance with the provisions of the Rohingya Ordinance of the International Court of Justice of 23 January 2020, to prevent the commission of any act referred to in article 2 of the Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide, including ensuring that no such act is committed by the army or by irregular armed units that may be under its authority or support or by organizations or persons that may be under its control, authority or influence, to prevent the destruction and to ensure the preservation of evidence and to report to the Court on any measures taken to give effect to the provisions of the Ordinance;

23. Stresses the need to address effectively the root causes of human rights violations and abuses against persons belonging to ethnic and religious minorities, including the Rohingya, and recalls the importance of the full implementation of all recommendations of the Consultative Commission on the Rakhine State, including those relating to access to nationality, freedom of movement, the elimination of systematic segregation and all forms of discrimination, and equitable and inclusive access to health services, education, livelihoods, basic services and birth registration, with full consultation with all minority ethnic and religious groups, including Rohingya, as well as vulnerable persons and civil society;

24. Also stresses the need to intensify efforts to eliminate statelessness and to eliminate systematic and institutionalized discrimination against members of ethnic and religious minorities, in particular the Rohingya, including the revision and reform of the Citizenship Act 1982, which has resulted in the denial of human rights, the complete deprivation of certain persons' rights and forced displacement, the equal access to citizenship as a whole through a transparent, voluntary and inclusive procedure and equal access to all civil and political rights by allowing self-identification, amendment or repeal of all discriminatory laws and policies, including the discriminatory provisions of the 2015 "Laws on the Protection of Race and Religion" on religious conversions, interfaith marriages, monogamy and birth control, and the repeal of all local decrees restricting the right to freedom of movement and access to civil status, health and education services and livelihoods;

25. Further stresses the need to restore the Rohingya and other population groups to their status as citizens of their own right and to the civil and political rights deriving from that status, including their free and equitable participation in elections and other democratic processes;

26. Calls for the creation of conditions that will enable internally displaced persons to return voluntarily and sustainably to their places of origin or to travel to places of their choice in safety and dignity, and for full access to humanitarian assistance to those in need, including all internally displaced persons, throughout the country, in full consultation with relevant individuals and the local population and also in consultation with relevant United Nations and civil society organizations, and recommends that women be consulted and represented at all levels of decision-making on the closure strategy and its implementation;

27. Urges all parties, in particular the Myanmar army, to ensure full respect for international humanitarian law and to allow and facilitate the free and unhindered access of local and international humanitarian personnel and other relevant international agencies throughout the country, including in order to ensure adequate assessment of needs and adequate supplies and equipment, to repeal the so-called Registration of Organizations Act, which impedes the delivery of humanitarian assistance, to eliminate the prosecution of persons and organizations on the basis of that Act, to respect and protect humanitarian personnel, including medical personnel, facilities, transport and equipment, in order to enable humanitarian organizations to provide humanitarian assistance to all those in need, including internally displaced persons, based on principles, inclusive and appropriate to the age, disability and gender of the beneficiaries;

28. Condemns the suspension and obstruction by the Myanmar army of humanitarian access to the Rakhine State following the passage of Cyclone Mocha and the cessation of the ceasefire concluded in November 2022 between the Myanmar Army and the Rakhine Army, and expresses its deep concern that the international humanitarian community, including United Nations agencies, humanitarian actors and the international media, continues to have very limited access to the affected areas of northern Rakhine State and other areas affected by the violence;

29. Calls for concrete measures to be taken to create conditions conducive to the safe and sustainable return of all refugees, including Rohingya and other forcibly displaced persons, who are in Bangladesh, recalling in this regard that a bilateral agreement on return was reached between Bangladesh and Myanmar in 2017, and those in other receiving States, and also calls for access to accurate and reliable information, which can be corroborated by the United Nations and other relevant actors, on the situation in the Rakhine State and other parts of the country, with a view to finding acceptable solutions to the major problems faced by all refugees, including Rohingya and other forcibly displaced persons, so that they may return to their places of origin or to their places of choice in safety and dignity, in a sustainable manner and through a voluntary approach;

30. Calls upon the international community to act in a genuine spirit of mutual assistance and equitable burden-sharing to assist Bangladesh in providing humanitarian assistance to Rohingya refugees and forcibly displaced persons until they are able to return voluntarily to Myanmar in safety and dignity and to contribute to the provision of humanitarian assistance in Myanmar to all affected persons belonging to an internally displaced community, including in the Rakhine State, taking into account the vulnerability of women, children, the elderly and persons with disabilities, and, inter alia, to provide adequate funding for the Joint Response Plan to the Rohingya Humanitarian Crisis and to take advantage of the opportunities created by the Global Refugee Forum held in 2023;

31. Requests that all United Nations human rights mandate-holders and mechanisms and human rights bodies and international and regional courts and tribunals have immediate access to the entire country, without restriction or monitoring, in order to independently monitor the human rights situation, including the elimination of Internet breaks and all other restrictions on access to the Internet, which impede the flow of information essential to the implementation of the principle of accountability, and that civil society organizations, human rights defenders, lawyers, victims, survivors, witnesses and other persons be given unimpeded access to and communicate with human rights entities, including those of the United Nations, without fear of being assaulted, intimidated or subjected to reprisals;

32. Also calls for an end to the reclassification of villages previously inhabited by Rohingya and other ethnic minorities, the removal of village names from official maps and any proposed land-use changes, and the immediate termination of the construction of military installations in the villages concerned;

33. Further requests that families be allowed to visit their families without hindrance again and that medical services and relevant international monitoring bodies have immediate and unwarranted access to detainees and places of detention;

34. Calls for full and unhindered access to members of the diplomatic corps, independent observers and representatives of the independent national and international media without fear of being assaulted, intimidated or subjected to reprisals;

35. Stresses the important role of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations in facilitating political dialogue, calls upon regional actors to work in the same direction, and calls upon all States Members of the United Nations to protect Myanmar nationals on their territory, as appropriate, and to respect the principle of non-refoulement;

36. Expresses deep concern that the Myanmar army has not made progress in the implementation of the five-point consensus of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations, reiterates the urgent call for Myanmar to implement fully, expeditiously and effectively this consensus, including through constructive dialogue

text_fr: entre toutes les parties concernées, afin de parvenir à une solution pacifique qui préserve les intérêts et les moyens de subsistance du Myanmar, demande à cette fin à toutes les parties prenantes  au  Myanmar  de  coopérer  avec  l'Association  et  avec  l'Envoyé  spécial  de  la présidence  de  l'Association  pour  le  Myanmar,  notamment  de  permettre  à  celui-ci  de s'entretenir avec toutes les parties prenantes, et exprime son soutien à ces efforts ;
text_en: between all parties concerned, in order to achieve a peaceful solution that preserves Myanmar's interests and livelihoods, calls upon all stakeholders in Myanmar to cooperate with the Association and with the Special Envoy of the Chair of the Association for Myanmar, in particular to allow the Association to meet with all stakeholders, and expresses its support for these efforts;

items_fr: 37. Appuie l'adoption de nouvelles mesures visant à l'application du consensus en cinq points de l'Association des nations de l'Asie du Sud-Est et, à cet égard, se félicite que les dirigeants de l'Association aient examiné cette application et adopté une décision à ce sujet au quarante-troisième sommet de l'Association, tenu le 5 septembre 2023 ;

38. Demande que  l'Organisation  des  Nations  Unies  joue  un  rôle  plus  actif  et recommande notamment que l'envoyé spécial du Secrétaire général pour le Myanmar soit rapidement nommé et qu'un poste de coordonnateur résident des organismes des Nations Unies au Myanmar soit créé à titre permanent, l'objectif étant d'améliorer la cohérence et l'efficacité de l'action sur le terrain, et que des mesures visant à amener l'armée à répondre de ses crimes continuent d'être prises ;

39. Souligne qu'il est urgent de protéger ceux qui signalent des violations et des atteintes et de faire cesser immédiatement les meurtres, les actes de torture et autres mauvais traitements,  les  atteintes  à  l'intégrité  physique  et  les  détentions  arbitraires  visant  tous  les acteurs de la société civile, y compris les journalistes et les professionnels des médias, les défenseurs  des  droits  de  l'homme,  les  personnes  chargées  de  recenser  les  victimes,  les avocats, les défenseurs de l'environnement et des droits fonciers, les professionnels de la santé, les travailleurs humanitaires et les civils en général ;

40. Demande que soient protégés le droit à la liberté de religion ou de conviction, le  droit  à  la  liberté  d'opinion  et  d'expression, le droit à la liberté de réunion pacifique et d'association et le droit à la vie privée, énoncés à l'article 12 de la Déclaration universelle des  droits  de  l'homme  et  à  l'article  17  du  Pacte  international  relatif  aux  droits  civils  et politiques, tant en ligne qu'hors ligne, et notamment que soient rétablies dans leur intégralité et de façon permanente toutes les formes de services d'accès à Internet dans l'ensemble du pays, que soient levées toutes les formes de censure en ligne, y compris les interdictions d'accès aux sites Web des médias et aux réseaux privés en ligne, ainsi que toutes les mesures visant  à  mettre  en  place  des  systèmes  de  surveillance  en  ligne,  y  compris  l'interception illégale  ou  arbitraire  des  communications,  la  collecte  illégale  ou  arbitraire  de  données personnelles, le piratage informatique illégal ou arbitraire et l'utilisation illégale ou arbitraire de technologies biométriques, que soient abrogées ou modifiées, conformément aux normes internationales  et  au  droit  international  des  droits  de  l'homme,  toutes  les  dispositions législatives pertinentes, en particulier la loi sur les secrets d'État, la loi sur les associations illégales, la loi sur les rassemblements et les manifestations pacifiques, les articles 66 (al. d)), 68 (al. a)), 77 et 80 (al. c)) de la loi sur les télécommunications, la loi sur la télévision et la radiodiffusion, la loi sur la protection de la vie privée et de la sécurité des citoyens, la loi sur les transactions électroniques, les articles 124A, 124C, 124D, 153, 295A, 499, 500 et 505 (al. a) et b)) du Code pénal et la loi sur l'administration des collectivités locales, et que soit adoptée une législation complète sur la protection des données ;

41. Demande également que les mesures nécessaires soient prises pour favoriser l'inclusion de toutes les personnes vivant au Myanmar et promouvoir le respect de leurs droits de l'homme et de leur dignité, pour lutter contre la violence sexuelle et fondée sur le genre, la  discrimination  et  la  propagation  de  préjugés,  notamment  la  diffusion  de  fausses informations, de discours haineux et de propos incendiaires, y compris sur les plateformes en ligne, les médias sociaux et les services de messagerie, et pour combattre l'incitation à la haine et à la violence à l'égard des minorités ethniques, religieuses et autres, y compris les Rohingya, conformément au Plan d'action de Rabat sur l'interdiction de l'appel à la haine nationale, raciale ou religieuse qui constitue une incitation à la discrimination, à l'hostilité ou à la violence et dans le droit fil de la recommandation n o 9 formulée dans le résumé du rapport final de la Commission d'enquête indépendante, et notamment qu'on s'abstienne de tenir des propos haineux ou d'encourager autrui à tenir de tels propos ainsi que de prendre des mesures qui sont censées lutter contre les discours de haine, mais ne sont pas conformes aux normes internationales ;

42. Demande en outre que des mesures soient prises pour combattre la violence sexuelle et fondée sur le genre liée au conflit au Myanmar et recueillir des informations à ce sujet,  avec  la  pleine  participation  de  la  société  civile,  en  particulier  les  organisations  de défense des droits des femmes et des femmes des communautés touchées par le conflit, ainsi que pour prévenir et éliminer cette violence, mettre fin à l'impunité, amener les auteurs à répondre de leurs actes et donner aux survivants accès à une assistance appropriée, à des services de soutien, à la justice et à des réparations ;

43. Souligne qu'il importe de consulter les survivants et les familles des victimes, y compris les Rohingya et les personnes appartenant à d'autres minorités, et de les associer aux mesures prises pour promouvoir la justice et l'établissement des responsabilités, selon qu'il conviendra ;

44. Demande que des mesures appropriées soient prises pour mettre fin au travail des  enfants  et  au  travail  forcé,  notamment  pour  donner  pleinement  suite  aux recommandations formulées par la Commission d'enquête de l'Organisation internationale du  Travail 3 ,  protéger  les  droits  des  travailleurs  du  secteur  de  l'extraction  des  ressources naturelles  et  démilitariser  les  régions  minières,  et  qu'on  s'emploie  à  établir  un  cadre  de gouvernance foncière inclusif et à régler les problèmes d'occupation des terres, notamment en modifiant la législation pertinente, en particulier la loi sur la gestion des terres vacantes, en jachère ou vierges, en pleine concertation avec les communautés ethniques et les groupes concernés, y compris les Rohingya ;

45. Engage toutes les entreprises, y compris les entreprises nationales et les sociétés transnationales  qui  sont  présentes  au  Myanmar  ou  y  ont  une  partie  de  leur  chaîne d'approvisionnement, à appliquer les Principes directeurs relatifs aux entreprises et aux droits de l'homme  et  les  recommandations  que  la  mission  internationale  indépendante d'établissement  des  faits  a  formulées  concernant  les  intérêts  économiques  de  l'armée  du Myanmar, engage toutes les sociétés transnationales à se comporter de manière responsable et à  rompre tout lien économique avec des entreprises appartenant à l'armée du Myanmar, et demande aux États d'origine de ces sociétés de renforcer les mesures visant à garantir que celles-ci font preuve d'une plus grande prudence encore en matière de droits de l'homme et veillent à ce que, conformément aux Principes directeurs, leurs activités ne viennent pas faciliter ni causer la perpétration de violations des droits de l'homme ou d'atteintes à ces droits ;

46. Décide de prolonger d'un an le mandat de Rapporteur spécial sur la situation des  droits  de  l'homme  au  Myanmar,  demande  au  Rapporteur  spécial  de  lui  présenter  un compte  rendu  oral  à  sa  cinquante-sixième  session  et  de  soumettre  un  rapport  écrit  à  la Troisième Commission à la soixante-dix-huitième session de l'Assemblée générale ainsi qu'à lui-même à sa cinquante-huitième session, conformément à son programme de travail annuel, et demande également au Rapporteur spécial de s'adjoindre le concours d'autres spécialistes des droits de l'homme pour continuer à suivre la situation des droits de l'homme au Myanmar et l'application des recommandations formulées par le titulaire du mandat et par la mission internationale  indépendante  d'établissement  des  faits  et  de  faire  des  recommandations, notamment  dans  des  rapports  thématiques  et  des  documents  de  séance,  concernant  les mesures supplémentaires à prendre pour remédier à la crise actuelle ;

47. Engage le  Rapporteur  spécial  à  continuer  de  dialoguer  avec  les  parties prenantes, dans la région et au-delà, dans le cadre de réunions et de conférences relatives aux droits de l'homme au Myanmar ;

48. Demande au  Myanmar  de  coopérer  immédiatement  et  pleinement  avec  le Rapporteur spécial aux fins de l'exercice de son mandat, notamment en facilitant ses visites et en lui accordant un accès sans restriction à l'ensemble du pays, et invite tous les États à faciliter la coopération avec le Rapporteur spécial, si nécessaire ;

49. Exhorte l'Organisation  des  Nations  Unies  à  veiller  à  ce  que  le  Mécanisme d'enquête indépendant pour le Myanmar dispose de l'appui, des effectifs, des installations et de  la  liberté  opérationnelle  dont  il  a  besoin  pour  s'acquitter  de  son  mandat  et  demande instamment  à  tous  les  organismes  des  Nations  Unies  de  collaborer  pleinement  avec  le
items_en: 37. Supports the adoption of further measures for the implementation of the five-point consensus of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations, and in this regard welcomes the review by the Association's leaders of this implementation and the adoption of a decision thereon at the forty-third summit of the Association, held on 5 September 2023;

38. Calls for a more active role for the United Nations, and recommends, inter alia, the early appointment of the Special Envoy of the Secretary-General for Myanmar and the establishment of a permanent United Nations resident coordinator post in Myanmar, with a view to improving the coherence and effectiveness of field action, and for continued efforts to hold the army accountable for its crimes;

39. Stresses the urgent need to protect those reporting violations and abuses and to put an immediate end to killings, torture and other ill-treatment, attacks on physical integrity and arbitrary detention of all civil society actors, including journalists and media professionals, human rights defenders, persons responsible for identifying victims, lawyers, environmental and land rights defenders, health professionals, humanitarian workers and civilians in general;

40. Calls for the protection of the right to freedom of religion or belief, the right to freedom of opinion and expression, the right to freedom of peaceful assembly and association and the right to privacy, as set out in article 12 of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights and article 17 of the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights, both online and offline, including the full and permanent restoration of all forms of Internet access services throughout the country, the lifting of all forms of online censorship, including the prohibitions of access to media websites and private online networks, as well as all measures aimed at establishing online surveillance systems, including illegal or arbitrary interception of communications, illegal or arbitrary collection of personal data, illegal or arbitrary piracy of illegal or arbitrary data, and the illegal or arbitrary use of biometric technologies, as well as all measures aimed at establishing relevant legislative provisions, including illegal or arbitrary interception of communications, illegal or arbitrary collection of illegal or arbitrary data, the removal of all forms of online censorship, in accordance 41. Also calls for the necessary measures to be taken to promote the inclusion of all persons living in Myanmar and to promote respect for their human rights and dignity, to combat sexual and gender-based violence, discrimination and the spread of prejudice, including the dissemination of false information, hate speech and inflammatory statements, including on online platforms, social media and messaging services, and to combat incitement to hatred and violence against ethnic, religious and other minorities, including the Rohingya, in accordance with the Rabat Plan of Action on the Prohibition of the Call for National, Racial or Religious Hate, which constitutes incitement to discrimination, hostility or violence and in line with recommendation No. 9 of the summary of the final report of the Independent Commission of Inquiry, including refraining from making hate speech or encouraging others to make such statements and taking measures that are supposed to combat hate speech, but are not in conformity with international standards;

42. Further calls for measures to combat and collect information on sexual and gender-based violence related to the conflict in Myanmar, with the full participation of civil society, in particular women's and women's rights organizations in conflict-affected communities, as well as to prevent and eliminate such violence, end impunity, hold perpetrators accountable and provide survivors with access to appropriate assistance, support services, justice and reparation;

43. Stresses the importance of consulting with survivors and families of victims, including Rohingya and persons belonging to other minorities, and of involving them in measures to promote justice and accountability, as appropriate;

44. Calls for appropriate measures to put an end to child labour and forced labour, including the full implementation of the recommendations of the Commission of Inquiry of the International Labour Organization 3 , the protection of the rights of workers in the natural resource extraction sector and the demilitarization of mining areas, and efforts to establish an inclusive land governance framework and to address land tenure issues, including by amending relevant legislation, in particular the Law on the Management of Vacant Land, Fallow Land and Virgin Land, in full consultation with ethnic communities and relevant groups, including the Rohingya;

45. Calls upon all enterprises, including national enterprises and transnational corporations that are present in Myanmar or have a part of their supply chain, to implement the Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights and the recommendations of the independent international fact-finding mission on the economic interests of the Myanmar army, calls upon all transnational corporations to behave responsibly and to break any economic links with companies belonging to the Myanmar army, and calls upon the States of origin of those companies to strengthen measures to ensure that they exercise even greater caution in the field of human rights and ensure that, in accordance with the Guiding Principles, their activities do not facilitate or cause human rights violations or abuses;

46. Decides to extend the mandate of the Special Rapporteur on the situation of human rights in Myanmar for one year, requests the Special Rapporteur to submit an oral report to the General Assembly at its fifty-sixth session and to submit a written report to the Third Committee at the seventy-eighth session of the General Assembly and to the General Assembly at its fifty-eighth session, in accordance with its annual programme of work, and also requests the Special Rapporteur to join other human rights experts in continuing to monitor the situation of human rights in Myanmar and the implementation of the recommendations made by the mandate holder and the independent international fact-finding mission and to make recommendations, including in thematic reports and conference room papers, on further measures to address the current crisis;
47. Encourages the Special Rapporteur to continue to engage with stakeholders in the region and beyond through human rights meetings and conferences in Myanmar;

48. Calls upon Myanmar to cooperate immediately and fully with the Special Rapporteur in the discharge of his mandate, including by facilitating his visits and granting him unrestricted access to the whole country, and invites all States to facilitate cooperation with the Special Rapporteur, if necessary;

49. Urges the United Nations to ensure that the Independent Inquiry Mechanism for Myanmar has the support, staffing, facilities and operational freedom it needs to fulfil its mandate, and urges all United Nations agencies to cooperate fully with the United Nations system in this regard.

text_fr: Mécanisme, dans la mesure du possible, et de communiquer les éléments de preuve pouvant être  utilisés  dans  le  cadre  de  poursuites  judiciaires,  et  à  tous  les  acteurs  concernés  du Myanmar ainsi qu'aux États de coopérer avec le Mécanisme, de lui donner accès aux témoins et à d'autres sources d'information, notamment, et de lui offrir toute l'assistance nécessaire à  l'exécution  de  son  mandat,  et  d'offrir  aux  victimes  et  aux  témoins  les  garanties  de confidentialité et de sécurité et le soutien nécessaires au plein respect du principe consistant à « ne pas nuire », et demande au Mécanisme de coopérer étroitement et en temps voulu à toutes  enquêtes  et  procédures  qui  pourront  être  menées  par  des  juridictions  nationales, régionales  ou  internationales,  notamment  la  Cour  pénale  internationale  et  la  Cour internationale de Justice ;
text_en: Mechanism, to the extent possible, and the provision of evidence that can be used in judicial proceedings, and to all relevant actors in Myanmar and States to cooperate with the Mechanism, to provide access to witnesses and other sources of information, inter alia, and to provide all necessary assistance to the Mechanism in the implementation of its mandate, and to provide victims and witnesses with the necessary guarantees of confidentiality and security and support for full respect for the principle of "no harm", and calls upon the Mechanism to cooperate closely and in a timely manner in any investigations and procedures that may be conducted by national, regional or international courts, including the International Criminal Court and the International Court of Justice;

items_fr: 50. Prie le Haut-Commissaire des Nations Unies aux droits de l'homme de suivre et d'évaluer, avec l'appui d'experts spécialisés, la situation générale des droits de l'homme au Myanmar, en s'intéressant particulièrement à l'établissement des responsabilités pour les violations présumées du droit international des droits de l'homme et du droit international humanitaire  et  à  l'état  de  droit,  de  suivre  l'application  de  la  présente  résolution  et  des résolutions antérieures portant le même titre, de formuler des recommandations concernant les  autres  mesures  à  prendre  pour  remédier  à  la  crise  actuelle  et  de  lui  présenter,  à  sa cinquante-neuvième  session,  un  rapport  complet  portant  notamment  sur  les  moyens  de réaliser les aspirations du peuple du Myanmar en ce qui concerne la protection des droits de l'homme,  l'établissement  des  responsabilités,  la  démocratie  et  l'instauration  d'un gouvernement civil, rapport dont la présentation sera suivie d'un dialogue ;

51. Prie également le Haut-Commissaire de s'intéresser en particulier à l'érosion de l'état de droit et aux effets de la crise sur les droits humains des civils, en particulier les journalistes, les femmes, les enfants, les défenseurs des droits de l'homme, les détenus et d'autres personnes, de fournir une assistance technique continue et renforcée aux personnes appartenant à ces groupes afin qu'elles puissent mieux se protéger, et de faire un point détaillé de la situation dans les rapports périodiques qu'il lui adressera, autant de tâches qu'il mènera avec l'appui d'experts et dans le cadre du suivi du rapport complet que le Haut-Commissariat lui présentera à sa cinquante-septième session ;

52. Recommande à l'Assemblée  générale  de  soumettre  les  rapports  du Haut-Commissaire et du Rapporteur spécial aux organes compétents de l'Organisation des Nations Unies, y compris le Conseil de sécurité, pour examen et suite à donner, en tenant compte des conclusions et recommandations qui y sont formulées ;

53. Réaffirme la  nécessité  d'établir  un  bureau  du  Haut-Commissariat  des Nations Unies aux droits de l'homme au Myanmar et d'adresser une invitation permanente à tous les titulaires de mandat au titre des procédures spéciales ;

54. Préconise la collaboration avec l'Envoyé spécial du Secrétaire général pour le Myanmar, dès sa nomination, de manière que l'intéressé puisse s'acquitter du mandat confié par le Secrétaire général, notamment se rendre dans le pays ;

55. Exhorte le  Haut-Commissaire  et  le  Rapporteur  spécial  à  surveiller  les violations des droits de l'homme qui se produisent en série et tendent à indiquer qu'il existe un  risque  particulièrement  élevé  de  crise  dans  le  domaine  des  droits  de  l'homme,  à communiquer les preuves de violations avec le Mécanisme d'enquête indépendant pour le Myanmar, lorsque cela est possible, afin de faciliter les poursuites, à continuer à porter ces informations à son attention en faisant apparaître l'urgence de la situation, notamment dans le  cadre de réunions d'information intersessions spéciales, à le conseiller sur les mesures supplémentaires qu'il pourrait avoir à prendre, conformément à son mandat de prévention, si la situation continuait à se détériorer, et à informer les autres organes de l'Organisation des Nations Unies, selon que de besoin, des progrès réalisés ;

56. Rappelle l'initiative  qu'a  prise  le  Secrétaire  général  d'adopter  des  mesures concrètes fondées sur les recommandations formulées dans le rapport intitulé « A brief and independent inquiry into the involvement of the United Nations in Myanmar from 2010 to 2018 » et invite le Secrétaire général à tirer parti de ce qui a déjà été accompli en formulant des recommandations permettant de prendre des mesures plus efficaces encore à l'avenir et de renforcer la capacité de prévention des organismes des Nations Unies au Myanmar ;

57. Prie le  Secrétaire  général  de  continuer  à  appeler  l'attention  du  Conseil  de sécurité  sur  la  situation  au  Myanmar  et,  en  tant  que  de  besoin,  à  formuler  des recommandations  concrètes  concernant  les  mesures  à  prendre  pour  résoudre  la  crise humanitaire, créer les conditions propices à un retour durable et librement consenti, dans la sécurité et dans la dignité, de tous les réfugiés et de toutes les personnes déplacées de force, y compris les Rohingya, et de garantir que les auteurs d'atrocités de masse, de violations des droits de l'homme et d'atteintes à ces droits seront amenés à répondre de leurs actes ;

58. Prie  également le  Secrétaire  général  de  fournir  au  Rapporteur  spécial,  au Haut-Commissaire et au Mécanisme d'enquête indépendant pour le Myanmar l'assistance, les ressources et les compétences supplémentaires dont ils ont besoin pour pouvoir s'acquitter pleinement de leur mandat ;
59. Décide de rester activement saisi de la question.
items_en: 50. Requests the United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights, with the support of specialized experts, to monitor and assess the overall human rights situation in Myanmar, with particular attention to accountability for alleged violations of international human rights law, international humanitarian law and the rule of law, to monitor the implementation of the present resolution and previous resolutions with the same title, to make recommendations on further measures to address the current crisis and to submit a comprehensive report to the General Assembly at its fifty-ninth session, including on ways and means of achieving the aspirations of the people of Myanmar with regard to the protection of human rights, accountability, democracy and the establishment of a civilian Government, followed by a dialogue;

51. Also requests the High Commissioner to pay particular attention to the erosion of the rule of law and the impact of the crisis on the human rights of civilians, in particular journalists, women, children, human rights defenders, detainees and others, to provide continuous and enhanced technical assistance to persons belonging to those groups in order to enhance their protection, and to provide a detailed account of the situation in his periodic reports to the General Assembly, which he will carry out with the support of experts and as part of the follow-up to the comprehensive report of the Office of the High Commissioner to the Assembly at its fifty-seventh session;

52. Recommends that the General Assembly submit the reports of the High Commissioner and the Special Rapporteur to the relevant organs of the United Nations, including the Security Council, for consideration and action, taking into account the conclusions and recommendations contained therein;
53. Reaffirms the need to establish an office of the Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights in Myanmar and to extend a standing invitation to all special procedures;

54. Encourages collaboration with the Special Envoy of the Secretary-General for Myanmar, upon appointment, so that he or she may fulfil the mandate entrusted to him or her by the Secretary-General, including visits to the country;

55. Urges the High Commissioner and the Special Rapporteur to monitor recurrent human rights violations and tends to indicate that there is a particularly high risk of a human rights crisis, to provide evidence of violations with the Independent Inquiry Mechanism for Myanmar, where possible, in order to facilitate prosecution, to continue to bring such information to his attention by highlighting the urgency of the situation, including through ad hoc intersessional briefings, to advise him on additional measures that he may need to take, in accordance with his preventive mandate, if the situation continues to deteriorate, and to inform other United Nations bodies, as appropriate, of progress made;

56. Recalls the initiative taken by the Secretary-General to adopt concrete measures based on the recommendations contained in the report entitled "A briefing and independent inquiry into the movement of the United Nations in Myanmar from 2010 to 2018", and invites the Secretary-General to build on what has already been achieved by making recommendations for further effective action in the future and for strengthening the preventive capacity of the United Nations system in Myanmar;

57. Requests the Secretary-General to continue to draw the attention of the Security Council to the situation in Myanmar and, as appropriate, to make concrete recommendations on the measures to be taken to resolve the humanitarian crisis, to create conditions conducive to the sustainable and voluntary return, in safety and dignity, of all refugees and forcibly displaced persons, including the Rohingya, and to ensure that perpetrators of mass atrocities, violations of human rights and abuses of those rights are held accountable;

58. Also requests the Secretary-General to provide the Special Rapporteur, the High Commissioner and the Independent Inquiry Mechanism for Myanmar with the additional assistance, resources and expertise necessary to enable them to discharge their mandate fully;
59. Decides to remain actively seized of the matter.
